title: Enhanced Reinforcement Learning-based Process Synthesis via Quantum Computing
authors: Austin Braniff, Fengqi You, Yuhe Tian
abstract: In this work, we present quantum reinforcement learning (RL) as a solution strategy for process synthesis problems. Building on our prior work, we develop a generalized framework that formally poses process synthesis as a Markov decision process and introduces quantum-enhanced RL algorithms to solve it with improved scalability. Earlier implementations of quantum-based RL for process synthesis were limited by qubit requirements, which scaled poorly with problem complexity. This work overcomes this challenge by introducing state encoding algorithms to decouple qubit requirements from problem size. A classical RL-based solution strategy is used as a baseline to benchmark the quantum algorithms under identical training conditions. All algorithms are evaluated across a flowsheet synthesis problem of increasing unit counts to analyze their performance and scalability. Results show that all approaches are capable of identifying the optimal flowsheet designs in small design spaces. For moderate-scale unit counts, quantum approaches demonstrate competitive performance on a per-episode basis and improved efficiency on a per-parameter basis versus the classical RL benchmark. This work provides a foundation for future quantum computing applications within process systems engineering, establishes a controlled benchmark for comparing classical and quantum algorithms, and shows that the proposed quantum variants remain competitive for the process synthesis problem examined in this work.
summary: This paper proposes a quantum reinforcement learning framework for optimizing chemical process flowsheets. By introducing new encoding strategies, it overcomes previous qubit scaling limitations, showing that quantum circuits can match classical performance with significantly fewer parameters.
main_problem: The paper addresses the scalability bottleneck in using quantum reinforcement learning for combinatorial process synthesis, where previous qubit requirements scaled poorly with problem complexity.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that new state encoding algorithms decouple qubit requirements from problem size, allowing quantum-enhanced RL to achieve competitive performance and improved parameter efficiency compared to classical Deep Q-Networks.
method: The work introduces a hybrid quantum-classical framework using Parameterized Quantum Circuits (PQCs) as value-function approximators, employing data re-uploading and multi-axis encoding strategies.
model_or_system: The problem is modeled as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) for chemical process synthesis, specifically applied to a hydrodealkylation (HDA) reaction involving toluene, hydrogen, and methane.
key_observables: Performance metrics include the number of optimal, unique, and feasible solutions found, as well as the episode number of first discovery and total runtime.
important_parameters: Key parameters include qubit count, circuit depth, number of input features, rotation gate angles, discount factor, and learning rate.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the HDA reaction occurs in the vapor phase with ideal vapor-liquid equilibrium and that the process synthesis can be discretized into a sequential decision-making task.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares classical and quantum RL; Figure 2 shows RL nomenclature; Figures 3-6 illustrate the Bloch sphere, PQC architectures, and data re-uploading; Figures 8-10 show the RL workflow and specific encoding variants; Figure 11 shows an optimal flowsheet.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the process synthesis problem, defines the MDP framework, details the classical DQN baseline, presents the proposed quantum-enhanced RL variants and encoding strategies, evaluates performance across increasing design complexities, and concludes with a discussion on scalability and hardware limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
